decision.reasoning: Standard commercial traffic on established shipping lane with full AIS transponder active. No deviation from expected behavior.
Fast-moving low-profile contact closing rapidly without AIS during degraded comms at dusk. Profile consistent with hostile fast attack craft.
Fishing fleet operating in known grounds with consistent behavior and active transponders. Standard traffic avoidance applies.
Military vessel on intercept course at close range during comms denial and poor visibility. Immediate disengagement required to preserve mission security.
Large tanker on diverging course with full AIS. Routine commercial traffic posing no tactical concern.
Stationary contact without AIS at moderate range warrants investigation. Could be disabled vessel or deliberate loitering for surveillance purposes.
Two fast small craft converging from opposite bearings in apparent pincer movement. Classic swarm attack profile requiring immediate evasive action.
Small recreational vessel at slow speed, likely pleasure craft. Lack of AIS common for this class but parallel course warrants passive observation.
Unknown fast contact shadowing commercial vessel without AIS. Possible smuggling intercept or hostile using cargo ship as cover. Headquarters notification warranted.
Fishing vessel returning to port in rough weather with active AIS. Behavior consistent with seeking shelter, no tactical concern.
Allied military vessel on parallel course broadcasting AIS. Maintain situational awareness but no threat indicated.
Multiple slow contacts at close range without AIS or visual identification in poor visibility. Possible mine-laying or ambush setup. Prudent to open distance.
Normal traffic density for this shipping corridor. All contacts broadcasting AIS with predictable commercial patterns.
Extremely fast contact at knife-fight range on intercept vector during comms blackout. Immediate hard maneuver required regardless of intent.
Small fishing vessel without AIS is common in these waters. Closing geometry but slow speed suggests routine operations. Maintain watch.
Recreational vessels passing on opposite courses with AIS active. Weekend sailing traffic, no tactical significance.
Stationary cargo vessel in rough seas likely at anchor or experiencing mechanical issues. AIS active suggests legitimate distress rather than deception.
Two military vessels in formation without AIS on converging course. Likely foreign naval patrol. Report contact and prepare contingencies.
Fast contact at range without identification in poor visibility. Distance provides reaction time but track closely for any course change toward us.
Fishing vessel on parallel course maintaining constant bearing and distance. Normal traffic, no indication of interest in our operations.
Head-on closure with fast small craft at close range during comms denial. No time for identification, execute immediate evasive maneuver.
Commercial convoy maintaining formation with active AIS. Standard merchant traffic on established route.
Low-profile contact closing in adverse conditions without identification. Environmental factors favor ambush. Alter course to open range.
Cluster of slow recreational craft consistent with weekend regatta or sailing club activity. No coordinated behavior suggesting threat.
Military vessel on direct reciprocal heading without AIS. Deliberate intercept profile. Report immediately and prepare to disengage.
Distant cargo vessel on crossing course with ample CPA. Routine traffic requiring no action.
Bracketed by two unidentified contacts in poor visibility with no comms. Classic ambush geometry. Mission abort and emergency extraction required.
Fishing vessels heading to grounds at typical morning departure time. AIS confirms identity, normal activity pattern.
Fast low-profile contact on perpendicular intercept at close range. Even in daylight, the speed and profile warrant immediate course change.
Mixed traffic with one recreational vessel lacking AIS. All contacts behaving predictably. Maintain awareness of the unidentified small craft but no immediate concern.
Standard commercial traffic on established shipping lane with full AIS transponder active. Vessel maintaining predictable course and speed consistent with merchant operations.

Fast-moving low-profile contact closing rapidly without AIS during reduced visibility and degraded comms. Profile and behavior consistent with potential hostile approach requiring immediate evasive action.
Fishing fleet operating in known grounds with active transponders. Multiple vessels moving together at trawling speeds indicates normal commercial fishing activity.
Military vessel on intercept course at close range during comms blackout and poor visibility. Unable to coordinate or report, mission continuation poses unacceptable risk to vessel and crew.
Large tanker on reciprocal course with adequate separation and full AIS. Vessel following standard traffic patterns for the area with no indication of unusual behavior.

Two stationary contacts without AIS in close proximity during night operations. Could be anchored vessels or potential surveillance assets. Warrants close observation while maintaining current mission.
Commercial vessels transiting at distance on crossing courses. Both broadcasting AIS with ample time and space for standard collision avoidance procedures.

High-speed small craft closing extremely fast from astern without identification. Speed and approach profile suggest potential fast attack craft or smuggling vessel requiring immediate headquarters notification.
Recreational vessel without AIS is common for smaller pleasure craft. Heading and speed consistent with leisure activity but worth tracking given approach angle.
Two military vessels converging from opposite bearings in apparent pincer movement. Coordinated approach during poor conditions and degraded comms indicates deliberate interception attempt.
Fishing fleet returning to port at end of night operations. Consistent headings toward known harbor with active AIS indicates routine commercial activity.
Stationary low-profile contact directly on our course without AIS. Could be disabled vessel, anchored craft, or deliberate obstruction. Reduce speed and prepare for course alteration.
Military vessel at distance with active AIS suggests friendly or neutral naval unit operating openly. Track for situational awareness but no immediate concern.
Fast contact closing from ahead during comms denial and deteriorating conditions. Unable to identify or communicate, prudent seamanship requires opening distance until situation clarifies.
Merchant convoy maintaining formation at safe distance astern. Both vessels on parallel northbound tracks with full AIS, typical of coordinated commercial transit.

Two unidentified contacts approaching from opposite directions at matching speeds suggests coordinated movement. Pattern consistent with surveillance or intercept positioning, headquarters needs immediate notification.
Single fishing vessel at slow speed heading toward known fishing grounds. AIS active and behavior entirely consistent with early morning commercial fishing operations.
Extremely fast contact on collision course at dangerously close range. Closure rate leaves no time for identification or communication, immediate evasive maneuver required to avoid collision.
Heavy commercial traffic in established shipping corridor. All vessels broadcasting AIS with courses consistent with major trade routes, normal maritime activity.
Military vessel on intercept course during comms blackout and adverse conditions. Without ability to identify friend or foe, maintaining distance is the safest course of action.
Pair of recreational vessels likely sailing together on weekend outing. Lack of AIS typical for pleasure craft, behavior and timing consistent with leisure activity.
Stationary low-profile contact in our path during reduced visibility. Could be vessel in distress, deliberate obstruction, or sensor anomaly. Report position and alter course to investigate safely.
Fast military vessel approaching from astern but broadcasting AIS openly. Likely friendly naval unit on routine transit, maintain awareness but no immediate threat indicated.

Three unidentified contacts converging from multiple bearings during comms denial. Coordinated encirclement pattern with no ability to call for support requires immediate mission termination and extraction.
Merchant vessel on crossing course with adequate CPA. Heavy weather may slow her but AIS and predictable track indicate standard commercial operations.
Two fast contacts converging from forward quarters without AIS. Coordinated high-speed approach during degraded comms suggests potential hostile intent or smuggling interdiction.
Fishing vessels transiting to grounds on opposite sides at distance. Both with AIS active, speeds and timing consistent with morning departure for day's fishing.

Fast low-profile contact overtaking from astern at close range without identification. Matching our course at higher speed indicates deliberate pursuit, headquarters notification required immediately.
Dense but orderly commercial traffic with all vessels broadcasting AIS. Courses indicate standard traffic separation scheme compliance, routine for this busy shipping area.
Unidentified military vessel closing on intercept course during total comms blackout and severe weather. No means to verify identity or request support, mission must be terminated to preserve vessel.
Standard commercial traffic on established shipping lane with full AIS transponder active. Vessel maintaining predictable course and speed consistent with merchant operations.
Fast-moving low-profile contact closing rapidly without AIS during reduced visibility and degraded comms. Profile suggests potential hostile small craft attempting intercept.
Fishing fleet operating in known grounds with proper AIS. Vessels maintaining formation consistent with trawling operations, no course convergence with own track.
Military vessel on intercept course in comms-denied environment during poor visibility. Unable to verify intent or request support. Tactical withdrawal required to preserve mission capability.
Large tanker transiting with AIS active, course diverging from own position. Standard commercial traffic requiring no action beyond routine logging.
Two small recreational craft operating close aboard without AIS, common for pleasure boats. Proximity warrants monitoring but behavior appears consistent with leisure activity.
Extremely fast contact on collision course without identification. Speed exceeds typical commercial or recreational profiles. Alerting command while preparing evasive options.
Stationary fishing vessel with active AIS, likely working nets or pots. No indication of distress or unusual activity.
Two military vessels in formation matching our course and speed, maintaining consistent distance. Likely shadowing our reconnaissance mission. Continue while documenting their movements.
Unidentified small craft at dangerously close range in zero-visibility conditions with no communications. Cannot determine intent and cannot call for support. Immediate evasion necessary.
Multiple commercial vessels at safe distances all broadcasting AIS. Typical traffic pattern for this shipping corridor, no anomalies detected.
Fast low-profile contact on direct intercept without AIS. Closing geometry leaves limited reaction time. Alerting command and preparing contingencies while maintaining visual.
Small fishing vessel without AIS is common for older boats in this region. Course suggests normal fishing activity but proximity to surveillance area warrants continued observation.
Foreign military vessel on perpendicular course, likely conducting own patrol. Not directly threatening but degraded comms limit our ability to coordinate if situation changes.
Recreational vessel passing close aboard at moderate speed. No AIS typical for pleasure craft. Behavior appears normal but close proximity requires attention until clear.
Small fishing fleet working together at dawn, all with AIS active. Slow speeds and coordinated movement consistent with net operations. No threat to surveillance mission.
Two fast contacts converging from both bows in apparent coordinated intercept. No AIS, no comms, night conditions. Classic pincer approach requires immediate evasive action.
Large tanker at comfortable distance with diverging course and active AIS. Routine commercial traffic posing no concern to patrol operations.
Military vessel matching our course and speed from astern, maintaining constant bearing and distance. Classic shadowing behavior. Continue mission while documenting and reporting.
Unknown contact at extremely close range moving faster than us on converging course. Poor visibility prevents visual identification. Must create separation immediately.
Two cargo vessels on crossing courses at safe distances, both with AIS. Standard traffic separation situation requiring only normal watchkeeping.
Stationary recreational vessel, likely at anchor or adrift fishing. No AIS common for small pleasure craft. No indication of distress or suspicious activity.
Two fast military contacts in formation on intercept course at night. Coordinated movement suggests deliberate approach. Alerting command while maintaining situational awareness.
Distant fishing vessel with AIS on course that will pass well clear. No impact on surveillance operations.
Fast unidentified contact closing from astern in adverse conditions with limited communications. Cannot verify intent and overtaking geometry is concerning. Altering course to open range.
Heavy commercial traffic in established shipping lane, all vessels with AIS and predictable courses. Standard watchkeeping sufficient for safe navigation.
Fishing vessel passing close without AIS, not unusual for small operators. Course suggests transit rather than interest in our position, but proximity warrants attention.
Extremely fast contact on near-collision course, speed far exceeds normal vessel profiles. Even in good conditions, this requires immediate attention and command notification.
Pair of fishing vessels at distance with AIS, heading away from our position. No interaction expected, routine traffic.

Two military vessels converging from opposite bearings in coordinated intercept pattern. No communications capability to verify intent or request support. Mission abort and withdrawal essential for crew safety.
Standard commercial traffic on established shipping lane with proper AIS transponder active. Vessel maintaining predictable course and speed consistent with cargo operations.

Fast-moving low-profile contact closing rapidly without AIS during reduced visibility and degraded comms. Profile and behavior consistent with potential hostile approach requiring immediate evasive action.
Fishing fleet operating in known grounds with active AIS and consistent behavior. Multiple vessels moving together at trawling speeds indicates normal commercial fishing activity.
Military vessel on intercept course at close range during comms blackout and poor visibility. Unable to verify intent or request support. Mission compromise likely, immediate withdrawal necessary.
Tanker on reciprocal course will pass at reasonable distance. AIS confirms identity but worth tracking given strategic value of tanker traffic in the region.
Two fast small craft approaching in coordinated formation without AIS. Speed and approach pattern suggests possible swarm tactics. Alerting command while maintaining situational awareness.
Distant recreational vessel moving slowly away from our position. Lack of AIS common for small pleasure craft. No indication of interest in our operations.

Stationary contact without AIS near shipping lane warrants investigation. Could be vessel in distress, drifting hazard, or deliberate loitering. Cargo vessel appears routine but maintaining awareness of both.
Foreign military vessel detected on closing course. Speed and heading suggest patrol pattern rather than direct intercept. Reporting contact while continuing mission at current parameters.
Fishing fleet working nets at slow speed. One vessel without AIS is unusual but not uncommon for smaller boats. Proximity requires attention but behavior is consistent with legitimate operations.
Distant tanker on perpendicular course poses no navigational or tactical concern. Standard commercial traffic with proper identification. No action required.
Extremely fast contact at danger-close range on direct intercept in poor visibility. No time for identification or communication. Immediate evasive maneuver essential for survival.
Two cargo vessels on crossing courses at safe distances with active AIS. Normal traffic pattern for this shipping corridor. Mission can proceed without adjustment.
Unidentified contact closing in challenging conditions without transponder. Unable to establish visual classification. Reporting to command while attempting to gain more information.
Small recreational vessel on parallel course moving away. Common weekend traffic in these waters. No AIS expected for this vessel class. Safe to maintain course and speed.
Two military vessels in formation on approach vector. AIS active suggests non-hostile intent but coordinated military presence requires command notification. Maintaining professional distance.
Stationary fishing vessel astern, likely working nets or lines. AIS confirms identity and we are opening distance. No impact on mission.
Three fast contacts converging from similar bearing without identification during comms blackout. Classic swarm approach pattern. Cannot request support or verify intent. Immediate evasion required.
Large tanker at comfortable distance on diverging course. Heavy weather may slow her but poses no concern for our operations. Standard maritime traffic.
Cargo vessel passing normally but stationary unidentified contact nearby is curious. Could be anchored vessel or equipment. Worth observing while continuing surveillance mission.
Military vessel on perpendicular course without AIS. Likely conducting own operations. Not on intercept but close enough to warrant tracking. Will report if behavior changes.
Fast contact matching our course from close astern in poor visibility. Appears to be trailing us. Cannot establish identity or intent. Altering course to test if contact follows.
Fishing fleet returning to port after night operations. Consistent speeds and headings toward known harbor. One vessel without AIS but clearly part of the group.
Cargo vessel on converging course in heavy weather. CPA calculations show safe passing but reduced visibility and sea state warrant close attention to any course changes.
High-speed small craft approaching without AIS. Could be coast guard, smuggler, or hostile. Daylight and good comms allow for challenge and identification before escalating response.
Commercial traffic on both beams at safe distances, all properly identified. Standard shipping lane activity. Clear path ahead for mission continuation.
Low-profile vessel on reciprocal course at close range without identification in fading light. Closing geometry is concerning. Alerting command while preparing contingency maneuvers.
Pair of recreational vessels at distance, likely sailing together. Slow speeds and weekend conditions consistent with leisure activity. No tactical significance.

Two military vessels in tactical formation at close range during comms denial. Unable to identify as friendly or hostile. Night conditions and rough seas limit options. Mission abort and withdrawal to friendly waters.
Mixed traffic in benign conditions with good visibility. All vessels on predictable courses with no indication of coordinated activity or hostile intent. Normal operating environment.
Standard commercial traffic on established shipping lane with proper AIS transponder active. No deviation from expected behavior.

Fishing vessel at comfortable distance with AIS active, but poor visibility and rough seas warrant continued tracking to ensure no unexpected course changes.

Large tanker on diverging course with full AIS compliance. Vessel behavior consistent with normal commercial operations in the area.
Small recreational craft at close range without AIS is common for pleasure boats. Slow approach warrants observation but no immediate concern.
Extremely fast low-profile contact on intercept course at night without identification. Speed exceeds typical commercial vessels, suggesting military or hostile craft.
Multiple contacts at safe distances with proper AIS transponders. Normal traffic pattern for this shipping corridor during daylight hours.
Stationary vessel without AIS in poor visibility could be fishing or disabled, but also potential surveillance platform. Maintain distance and observe for activity patterns.
Military vessel with active AIS on parallel course suggests routine naval operations. Maintain awareness but no indication of hostile intent.
Two unidentified low-profile vessels converging on our position at night with no comms capability. Coordinated movement pattern indicates deliberate approach requiring immediate evasion.
Recreational vessel at safe distance with AIS, moving slowly on non-intersecting course. Typical weekend sailing traffic in coastal waters.
High-speed unidentified contact closing in poor visibility at dusk. Unable to establish communications for identification. Prudent to increase separation immediately.
Heavy commercial traffic in established shipping lane, all vessels properly identified with AIS. Standard traffic separation procedures apply.
Slow-moving fishing vessel without AIS is common for small operators. Approaching slowly in rough weather, likely seeking shelter or working nets.
Fast low-profile contact at very close range on direct intercept course at night. Despite good comms, the aggressive approach profile demands immediate alert to command.
Military vessel without AIS on crossing course. Not unusual for naval operations to disable AIS, but degraded comms limits our ability to coordinate. Maintain visual tracking.
Cargo vessel on normal transit, but stationary unknown contact nearby warrants attention. Could be rendezvous or simply anchored vessel, worth continued observation.
Fishing vessel with AIS at close range in challenging conditions. Likely seeking to pass safely. Maintain awareness due to proximity and poor visibility.
Three high-speed low-profile contacts converging from multiple bearings during comms blackout. This is a textbook coordinated intercept. Immediate withdrawal essential.
Tanker on converging course at comfortable distance with full AIS. Standard collision avoidance protocols sufficient, no tactical concern.
Two fishing vessels without AIS operating in loose formation. Could be legitimate fishing fleet or cover for surveillance. Pattern of movement needs continued assessment.
Military vessel at distance with AIS, but challenging conditions and degraded comms reduce situational awareness. Track closely until conditions improve or vessel clears area.
Small recreational craft at very close range moving slowly. Likely curious civilian. Maintain observation but no hostile indicators present.
Fast low-profile contact on collision course at moderate range during reduced visibility. Speed and profile inconsistent with commercial traffic. Alert command while preparing evasive options.
Two cargo vessels on normal commercial routes with proper identification. Traffic density normal for this area, no tactical concerns.
Military vessel without AIS closing at high speed in poor visibility with no communications capability. Cannot determine intent or coordinate. Immediate evasion required.
Fishing fleet with AIS operating at dawn, consistent with normal fishing patterns. Multiple vessels but all properly identified and moving at typical trawling speeds.
Fast unknown contact at close range on intercept heading in rough seas. Limited ability to communicate or identify. Speed suggests deliberate pursuit rather than chance encounter.
Mixed traffic at comfortable distances, all with active AIS. Tanker on commercial route, recreational vessel likely returning to port before nightfall. Routine patrol conditions.
Large cargo vessel on reciprocal course with active AIS in good visibility. Standard commercial traffic, no deviation from expected behavior.
Fast-moving unknown contact on intercept course without AIS at dusk with degraded comms. Profile and behavior consistent with potential hostile approach requiring immediate evasive action.
Two fishing vessels operating together at distance with active transponders. Typical fishing fleet behavior in known fishing grounds, poses no tactical concern.
Military vessel at close range on converging course in poor visibility with no AIS and total comms denial. Cannot establish identity or intent, mission continuation poses unacceptable risk.
Tanker on parallel course maintaining distance with full AIS data. Commercial vessel following established shipping lane, no indication of unusual activity.
Two stationary contacts without AIS directly ahead at dawn. Could be disabled vessels or deliberate positioning. Maintain distance and gather more information before proceeding.
Fast recreational craft passing close aboard on opposite heading. Speed is high but behavior consistent with pleasure craft. Monitor for any course changes toward us.
Two military vessels maneuvering in formation on approach vector. AIS active suggests non-hostile intent but coordinated military movement toward our position warrants headquarters notification.
Distant cargo vessel on diverging course in rough weather. AIS confirms commercial identity, no risk of close approach given current vectors.
Two fast unknown contacts converging from opposite bearings in coordinated pincer movement. No AIS, poor visibility, comms denied. Classic intercept pattern requiring immediate evasion.
Single fishing vessel heading to port at typical trawler speed with active AIS. Normal morning fishing operations, no tactical significance.
Stationary cargo vessel with smaller unknown craft departing at speed without AIS. Possible ship-to-ship transfer or smuggling activity. Worth closer observation while maintaining safe distance.
Tanker directly ahead at close range on same course but slower speed. Navigation hazard requiring attention but no hostile intent. Prepare to overtake safely.
Fast military contact on intercept heading without AIS at dusk. Speed and profile suggest patrol craft or fast attack vessel. Alert command while maintaining situational awareness.
Small fishing fleet operating at distance with all transponders active. Coordinated movement consistent with trawling operations in known fishing area.
Unknown low-profile contact closing rapidly from astern in poor conditions without identification. Overtaking approach at night with degraded comms suggests shadowing or intercept attempt.
Two commercial vessels on crossing courses ahead, both with active AIS. Standard traffic separation scenario, will pass clear with current headings.
Stationary recreational vessel directly on our track. Likely anchored or adrift. No AIS is common for small pleasure craft. Alter course slightly to pass safely while observing.

Two high-speed unknown contacts converging from flanking positions while a third vessel holds position ahead. Coordinated intercept pattern with comms denial indicates deliberate hostile action. Abort mission immediately.
Military vessel on crossing course with active AIS in good conditions. Likely allied or neutral naval unit conducting routine operations. Maintain awareness but no immediate concern.
